```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "An existing logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier assignment with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments (>500kg) across multiple carriers without supervisor approval.",
    "bounded_context": "Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "A unit of freight to be delivered, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier at dispatch time." },
      { "term": "Carrier", "definition": "The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment." },
      { "term": "Carrier Assignment", "definition": "The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Currently an invariant — locked once set, changeable only by a Supervisor." },
      { "term": "Supervisor", "definition": "A privileged role authorized to reassign Carriers, the only actor permitted to break a Carrier Assignment lock." },
      { "term": "Sub-Shipment", "definition": "A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does not exist in the current domain model." },
      { "term": "Auto-Split", "definition": "Proposed automatic process that decomposes a Shipment exceeding 500kg into multiple sub-shipments, each with its own Carrier." },
      { "term": "Dispatch", "definition": "The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The invariant checkpoint where exactly-one-Carrier is enforced." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a data/state integrity constraint. Violating it means a Shipment could be unowned (lost freight) or multi-owned (conflicting delivery attempts, duplicate billing, chain-of-custody collapse). The system's correctness depends on this 1:1 relationship.",
      "testable_assertion": "At any point in time, COUNT(Carriers assigned to a single Shipment) == 1."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an authorization and state-integrity constraint. Without it, automated processes or unauthorized actors could silently reroute freight — breaking chain-of-custody, audit trails, and contractual obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any Carrier reassignment event on a locked Shipment must have an associated Supervisor actor ID, or the operation is rejected."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "At dispatch time, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the dispatch-gate invariant. A Shipment without a Carrier cannot physically move. A Shipment with multiple Carriers creates conflicting pickup obligations. Dispatch is the last enforcement checkpoint before physical world consequences.",
      "testable_assertion": "The dispatch operation fails if the Shipment's assigned Carrier count != 1."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A Shipment is exactly 500kg", "expected_behavior": "Must NOT trigger auto-split — the threshold is 'exceeds 500kg', not 'equals or exceeds'. But this boundary needs explicit product confirmation." },
    { "scenario": "A Shipment is 501kg but cannot be logically divided (e.g., single indivisible item)", "expected_behavior": "Auto-split is physically impossible. The feature must handle indivisible freight that exceeds the weight threshold." },
    { "scenario": "Auto-split produces a sub-shipment that itself exceeds 500kg", "expected_behavior": "Recursive splitting? Or is the 500kg threshold only at the parent level? Unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "A Carrier was already assigned to the parent Shipment before the split occurs", "expected_behavior": "The existing locked assignment must be dealt with. Who authorizes removing the original Carrier? This is exactly the Supervisor-only invariant being violated." },
    { "scenario": "One sub-shipment is delivered but another fails", "expected_behavior": "Partial delivery of a single logical Shipment. Who is liable? How is the customer notified? What is the SLA impact? The original Shipment concept assumed atomic delivery." },
    { "scenario": "Customer disputes charges after a split — they booked one Shipment but are billed for multiple", "expected_behavior": "Billing model is entirely unaddressed. Multi-carrier splits likely change pricing, insurance, and liability allocation." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Multi-carrier splits fundamentally change the cost structure. A single Shipment has one carrier rate; splitting introduces multiple carrier contracts, potentially higher aggregate cost, and a billing model that doesn't exist yet. Insurance and liability allocation across sub-shipments is undefined.",
    "operational": "This introduces a new orchestration layer — tracking, delivery coordination, partial-failure handling, and reconciliation across sub-shipments. Operational complexity increases significantly. Every downstream system that assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier (tracking, billing, notifications, SLA monitoring, reporting) must be audited.",
    "user": "Customers who booked a single Shipment will now see multiple tracking numbers, potentially multiple delivery windows, and a fragmented delivery experience. This is a fundamental change to the customer contract, not a transparent optimization.",
    "risk": "HIGH. The feature as specified directly violates two production invariants (BR-1, BR-2). Implementing it without resolving the contradiction risks corrupting the domain model, breaking audit trails, and creating undefined states in chain-of-custody. Additionally, bypassing Supervisor authorization for carrier changes may violate regulatory or contractual obligations.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Must now coordinate multi-carrier pickups for a single logical shipment. Dispatch workflows, scheduling, and capacity planning all change." },
      { "group": "Supervisors", "impact": "Their authority over carrier assignment is being bypassed by automation. This may be intentional but contradicts the current trust model and audit requirements." },
      { "group": "Carriers", "impact": "Receive partial loads from split shipments. Contract terms, SLAs, and pickup coordination change." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Delivery experience fragments. Single shipment becomes multiple deliveries. Tracking, communication, and expectations all affected." },
      { "group": "Finance / Billing", "impact": "Pricing model must account for split shipments. Revenue recognition, invoicing, and cost allocation all need new logic." },
      { "group": "Compliance / Legal", "impact": "Chain-of-custody splits across carriers. Liability allocation for damage/loss in transit becomes ambiguous. Regulatory reporting that assumes 1:1 Shipment:Carrier may break." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "The feature request directly contradicts two established production invariants. The spec provides no resolution strategy for this contradiction — it simply asks to violate the invariants 'without supervisor involvement.' Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to (a) change the domain model, (b) introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup), or (c) redefine what 'Shipment' means, acceptance criteria cannot be safely defined. Confidence is low because shipping wrong acceptance criteria here would corrupt the domain.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The contradiction between the feature request and BR-1/BR-2 is real and must be resolved before implementation",
      "Sub-Shipment is a new domain concept that does not exist in the current model",
      "Downstream systems assuming 1:1 Shipment:Carrier will be affected"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the product team intends to change the invariants or work around them",
      "The billing, insurance, and liability model for split shipments",
      "Whether 'without supervisor involvement' is a hard requirement or a preference",
      "Whether the 500kg threshold is a business policy or a physical/regulatory constraint"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are currently enforced in production and relied upon by other systems",
      "The product team may not have fully considered the invariant violations implied by this request"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Shipment = one Carrier). Splitting a Shipment across multiple carriers means either: (A) the invariant is relaxed and a Shipment can have N carriers, fundamentally changing the domain model, or (B) a new aggregate concept (e.g., ShipmentGroup or ParentShipment) is introduced where each sub-shipment still respects the 1:1 invariant. These are architecturally and domain-wise completely different solutions.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations", "Carriers", "Customers", "Finance", "Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "Option A: Relax BR-1 to allow many-to-many Shipment:Carrier. Requires auditing every system that depends on the 1:1 invariant.",
        "Option B: Introduce a ShipmentGroup aggregate. Parent Shipment splits into child Sub-Shipments, each with exactly one Carrier. BR-1 is preserved at the Sub-Shipment level. Parent becomes a coordination/tracking entity.",
        "Option C: Reject the feature as specified. Require overweight shipments to be manually split by a Supervisor, preserving all existing invariants."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — introduce a ShipmentGroup/ParentShipment aggregate. This preserves BR-1 at the sub-shipment level, introduces the split concept cleanly, and avoids corrupting the existing domain model. But it requires product to define the customer-facing semantics of a 'split shipment.'"
    },
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The feature explicitly requests bypassing Supervisor authorization ('without requiring supervisor involvement'), which directly violates BR-2. The Supervisor lock exists to maintain chain-of-custody integrity and audit compliance. Bypassing it for automated splits means either: (A) the system acts with Supervisor-equivalent authority (who is accountable?), or (B) auto-split is a different operation than reassignment and needs its own authorization model.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Supervisors", "Compliance", "Operations"],
      "options": [
        "Option A: Auto-split operates under a system-level Supervisor identity, maintaining the audit trail with a 'System:AutoSplit' actor.",
        "Option B: Define auto-split as a distinct domain operation separate from reassignment, with its own authorization rules and audit trail.",
        "Option C: Require Supervisor approval for splits, contradicting the feature request but preserving the invariant."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — auto-split should be modeled as a distinct domain event (ShipmentSplit), not as a carrier reassignment. This avoids conflating two different business operations and allows each to have appropriate authorization rules. But the product team must confirm that bypassing Supervisor authority is truly the intent."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Feature requires a single Shipment to be handled by multiple Carriers simultaneously", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier" },
    { "violation": "Feature bypasses Supervisor authority for carrier changes via automated splitting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment" }
  ]
}
```

**Bottom line:** This feature request **cannot be accepted as specified**. It directly contradicts two production invariants. The acceptance criteria array is intentionally empty because defining criteria against a contradictory spec would be worse than having none — it would give the team false confidence to build something that corrupts the domain.

The recommended path forward is **Option B from both escalations**: introduce a **ShipmentGroup** aggregate and model auto-split as a **distinct domain event**, not a carrier reassignment. This preserves the 1:1 Carrier invariant at the sub-shipment level and creates a clean authorization model for the split operation. But this requires the product team to come back with answers on billing, customer experience, liability allocation, and whether bypassing Supervisors is truly a hard requirement.
